title: Visual-to-Code Authoring, Tensor-Network Debugging, and Quantum-Circuit Inspection Tools in Python
abstract: Tensor networks and quantum circuits are structural objects whose meaning depends on connectivity, indices, contraction order, gate placement, measurements, and related design choices. They are often easier to reason about visually than as code, yet in Python they are frequently constructed, transformed, and checked through backend-specific objects or compact symbolic expressions. This can make structural mistakes hard to notice during development, debugging, and communication. This paper presents three complementary packages: Tensor-Network-Visualization for visual debugging and structural inspection of supported tensor-network and traced einsum workflows; Tensor-Network-Editor for visual-to-code authoring, backend code generation, JSON preservation, export, and design-level analysis; and Quantum Circuit Drawer for clear circuit rendering, inspection, and complementary comparison of circuits or documented result distributions. The packages form a visual authoring and inspection layer around existing tensor-network libraries, array-based scientific Python workflows, and quantum SDKs. They are not simulators: they do not implement new contraction algorithms, execute quantum circuits, or guarantee full semantic equivalence across arbitrary backends. Their contribution is to make structural artifacts visible, editable, inspectable, comparable, exportable, and reproducible within those ecosystems.
summary: This paper presents three Python packages designed to solve the problem of debugging and authoring complex structures like tensor networks and quantum circuits. These tools provide a visual layer for inspecting connectivity, editing designs visually, and comparing results without replacing the underlying computational backends. They are invaluable development aids for researchers working with these structural models.
main_problem: Structural mistakes are hard to notice when tensor networks and quantum circuits are represented as backend-specific Python objects or compact symbolic expressions.
main_result: The paper introduces three complementary packages—Visualization, Editor, and Drawer—to create a visual authoring and inspection layer around existing scientific libraries.
method: The tools provide structural normalization, allowing users to visually inspect, edit, compare, and export tensor network structures and quantum circuits without implementing new simulation algorithms.
model_or_system: The focus is on abstract computational structures: tensor networks (e.g., MPS, MERA) used in physics simulations, and quantum circuits defined by gates and connectivity.
key_observables: Structural correctness, network connectivity, contraction paths, circuit topology, result distribution histograms, and code snippets for various backends.
main_assumptions: The packages are explicitly *not* simulators; they do not implement new contraction algorithms or guarantee full semantic equivalence across arbitrary backends.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the architecture diagrams for the three main tools (Visualization, Editor, Drawer) and show representative views of tensor network structures and circuit inspection capabilities.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of structural ambiguity in modern quantum/tensor computation, then details the three complementary packages: Visualization (for debugging), Editor (for visual-to-code authoring), and Drawer (for circuit rendering). The structure emphasizes the utility of these tools for inspection and reproducibility.
why_interesting: While focused on tooling rather than physics results, providing robust visualization and structural analysis tools is crucial for developing complex quantum algorithms or simulating many-body systems using tensor networks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Architecture of tensor-network-visualization. Supported backend-native networks, traced einsum workflows, and direct tensor data enter through public inspection, normalization, rendering, export, and translation APIs. Structural inputs are dispatched through backend graph builders into a normalized tensor graph for 2D/3D rendering, optional contraction playback, snapshot export, and selected translation targets, while tensor-data inspection and tensor comparison form parallel public-API branches.

Figure 2: Architecture of tensor-network-editor. Editor sessions, CLI/Python builders, templates, supported imports, and optional limited live imports produce a backend-independent NetworkSpec design model. The model primarily drives backend code generation, while validation, linting, analysis, contraction planning, static export, and versioned JSON preservation support the visual-to-code authoring workflow.

Figure 3: Architecture of quantum-circuit-drawer. Panel (a) shows the circuit workflow: supported circuit objects, OpenQASM inputs, topology configuration, and public drawing/analysis calls are normalized through adapter and parser paths into CircuitIR-based preparation for 2D/3D rendering, circuit analysis, circuit comparison, and LaTeX export. Panel (b) shows the sibling result-distribution workflow: counts, quasi-probabilities, arrays, marginals, and supported result objects are normalized for histogram plotting and distribution comparison; this path visualizes and compares results but does not execute circuits.

Figure 4: Representative views from the media set associated with tensor-network-visualization version 2.0.3: an inspection-oriented network view, an MPS and contraction-oriented example, a MERA-style layout, and a tubular rendering, illustrating the package’s range from structural debugging to richer geometry-aware inspection.

Figure 5: Representative local-editor views from the tensor-network-editor README gallery for version 1.0.1: the main authoring canvas with generated code, template and reusable-subnetwork tooling, hyperedge and metadata editing, and the manual contraction planner. The gallery illustrates the interactive visual authoring interface; the executable example in the text isolates the shared design-to-code path.
